1: The superscription connects Psalm 34 to David's flight to Gath and his feigned madness before the Philistine king (1 Samuel 21:10–15). However, that narrative names the king "Achish," while this superscription says "Abimelech" — a discrepancy long noted by commentators. The most plausible explanation is that Abimelech ("my father is king") functioned as a dynastic title for Philistine rulers, much as Pharaoh designated Egyptian kings generically, and Agag was a title for Amalekite kings. This is supported by Genesis 20:2 and Psalms 26:1, where Abraham and Isaac both encounter someone named Abimelech in similar contexts centuries apart. The psalm itself is a carefully structured alphabetic acrostic: each of the 22 verses begins with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet (aleph through taw), a wisdom teaching device signaling comprehensive coverage of the subject.
2: "My soul boasts in the LORD" — the verb hāllal (to boast, praise, shine) is the root of hallĕlûyāh. The distinction here is between self-boasting (hāllal benapšô), which is condemned in the Psalter (Psalms 52:1; Psalms 94:4), and boasting in the LORD (hāllal bYHWH, cf. Jeremiah 9:24; 1 Corinthians 1:31), which is the right orientation of praise. "The humble (ʿănāwîm) will hear and be glad" — the ʿănāwîm are the pious poor, those who have internalized their dependence on God; this is a social and spiritual category that became central to Second Temple piety (the ʿănāwîm of Isaiah 61, the "poor in spirit" of the Beatitudes).
3: "Magnify the LORD with me and let us exalt his name together" — the invitation to communal praise is characteristic of thanksgiving psalms performed at the temple following personal deliverance. The thanksgiving psalm had a social function: the worshipper who had been delivered would bring an offering and testify publicly in the temple courts, inviting the assembled community to join in praise (cf. Psalms 22:22–25; Psalms 116:12–14). "His name" — the divine name carried the accumulated weight of all YHWH's prior acts of deliverance; to exalt the name is to declare the character it represents.
4: "I sought the LORD (dāraštî) and he answered me" — dāraš (seek, inquire) is the standard term for consulting the sanctuary oracle or petitioning the temple deity. In ancient Israel, "seeking the LORD" could mean physically going to the sanctuary, consulting a priest, or earnest prayer. The verb here functions as a testimony formula: the speaker reports what happened when they sought YHWH in prayer. "He delivered me from all my fears (mĕgûrôtāy)" — māgôr (fear, dread, terror) from gûr (to sojourn, be a stranger); the fears of one who is without protection in a hostile environment — precisely the David-in-Gath situation of the superscription.
5: "Those who look to him are radiant (nāhărû); their faces will not be ashamed" — nāhar means to shine or to stream (as a river); both meanings carry nuance here. The radiance of those who look to YHWH contrasts with the "shining" of the proud who glorify themselves. Face-shame (bōšet pānîm) in ANE honor-shame culture was the most devastating social consequence — public humiliation before one's community. The promise is not merely internal dignity but vindication in the eyes of the community, the restoration of social honor.
6: "This poor man (ʿānî) cried and the LORD heard" — the verse shifts abruptly to third person, creating a slight puzzle. Many interpreters read it as the psalmist speaking of himself in the third person (distancing himself from his own abasement), which was common in the Psalter and in ANE royal inscriptions. Others read it as a general statement about the pious poor inserted into the personal testimony. The shift from "I" (Psalms 34:3–5) to "this poor man" highlights that the psalmist's experience is paradigmatic — it illustrates what the LORD does for all who call on him in their poverty.
7: "The angel of the LORD (malʾak YHWH) encamps around those who fear him and rescues them" — the malʾak YHWH (messenger/angel of YHWH) is a distinctive presence in the Hebrew Bible who speaks as YHWH himself (Genesis 16:7–13; Psalms 22:11–18; Exodus 3:2; Joshua 5:13–15; Judges 2:1–4). This figure simultaneously represents and is identified with YHWH, leading to later theological reflection on divine presence and mediation. The imagery here is military: the angel "encamps" (ḥōneh) like a protective garrison around those who fear God — one warrior deity providing a protective perimeter around the pious.
8: "Taste and see that the LORD is good!" — one of the Psalter's most vivid sensory invitations. "Taste" (ṭaʿamû) may carry a specific liturgical reference: in the peace offering (šĕlāmîm) worship, the worshipper ate portions of the sacrifice in the temple courts as a feast of communion with God. To eat of the sacrifice was to participate in the divine table — the taste of the offering was meant to convey the goodness of the one worshipped. The invitation to "taste and see" thus may be a literal invitation to join the communal sacrificial feast, in which divine goodness was experienced through the physical act of eating. Ṭôb (good) — divine goodness is both moral excellence and practical beneficence toward those who seek him.
9: "Fear the LORD, you his holy people (qĕdōšāyw)" — qādôš (holy, set apart) applied to the human worshippers of YHWH designates those who belong to the divine sphere through covenant relationship. The qĕdōšîm are the covenant community understood as YHWH's holy ones, distinct from the nations. "Those who fear him lack nothing" (ʾên maḥsôr lîrēʾāyw) — the comprehensive sufficiency of divine provision for the covenant community; the same claim as Ps 23 ("I shall not want"). The statement is empirical-covenantal: those within the covenant structure experience its material and spiritual provisions.
10: "Young lions (kĕpîrîm) go hungry and lack food" — the young lion is the apex predator, the creature most capable of obtaining its own food by strength and violence. The shocking reversal: even the mightiest of natural hunters may go without, while "those who seek the LORD lack no good thing." This antithesis situates covenant piety above the food chain itself. In ANE wisdom literature, the power of the strong against the weak was taken as a natural fact; Psalm 34 subverts this by making YHWH's provision superior to the strength of the predator.
11: "Come, children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the LORD" — this verse signals the explicit wisdom-teaching register of the psalm. The address "children" (bānîm, sons/students) matches the teacher-student address of Proverbs (Proverbs 1:8; Psalms 2:1; Psalms 3:1; Psalms 4:1; Psalms 5:1; Psalms 7:1). The psalm shifts from testimony (Psalms 34:1–10) to instruction (Psalms 34:11–22), making explicit the pedagogical function of the thanksgiving form: the public testimony becomes a teaching event. "The fear of the LORD" is the wisdom tradition's master concept — not paralytic terror but reverential orientation toward God as the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 1:7; Psalms 9:10; Psalms 111:10).
12: "Who is the person who desires life (ḥayyîm) and loves long days to enjoy what is good?" — the wisdom question in rhetorical form, expecting the answer "all of us." In the wisdom tradition, long life and enjoyment of good are the fruits of righteous living (Proverbs 3:1–2, 16; Deuteronomy 5:16; Psalms 6:2; Psalms 11:9). "Life" here is not merely biological existence but flourishing existence — what the OT calls šālôm: wholeness, integration, prosperity within a good life. The question sets up the instruction of Psalms 34:13–14 as the path to this desired outcome.
13: "Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from speaking lies" — speech ethics is a major domain of Second Temple wisdom. Ben Sira (Ecclesiasticus) devotes multiple chapters to the dangers of the tongue (Sir Psalms 14:1; Psalms 19:4–17; Psalms 22:27–Psalms 23:15; Psalms 28:12–26). In the social world of the ancient village, false testimony, slander, and deceptive speech could destroy a person's reputation, legal standing, and economic livelihood. The "lying lips" (śĕpātayim mibbĕr mirmāh) echo the legal context of false witness in Exodus 20:16 and the covenant curses of Deut 27–28.
14: "Turn from evil and do good; seek peace (šālôm) and pursue it" — the two movement verbs are emphatic: sûr (turn away, remove oneself) and rādap (pursue, chase, hunt). The pursuit of šālôm requires active chase, not passive waiting. Šālôm in its full semantic range is not merely absence of conflict but wholeness, completeness, right relationship between persons and with God. This verse was prominent in Second Temple Judaism as a summary ethical instruction (cf. 1 Peter 3:11, which quotes it, and its echo in the Beatitude "blessed are the peacemakers").
15: "The LORD's eyes are on the righteous and his ears are open to their cry" — the divine senses directed toward the righteous: eyes (watchful care) and ears (responsive attention). This anthropomorphic language maps onto the temple theology in which the divine presence "watches" and "hears" from the sanctuary. Solomon's prayer at the temple dedication (1 Kings 8:29) uses identical language: "May your eyes be open toward this temple night and day, this place of which you said, 'My Name shall be there,' so that you will hear the prayer your servant prays toward this place."
16: "The LORD turns his face (pānāyw) against those who do evil, to cut off the memory of them from the earth" — the divine "face" directed against someone is the most severe form of divine opposition (Leviticus 17:10; Psalms 20:3, 5, 6; Psalms 26:17; Ezekiel 14:8; Psalms 15:7). This inverts the Aaronic blessing in which the divine face is lifted upon the worshipper (Numbers 6:25–26). "Cutting off the memory" — the same eradication formula used throughout the Psalter and Deuteronomy for enemies; in ANE culture, to have one's name and memory cut off was the ultimate defeat, equivalent to non-existence.
17: "The righteous cry out (ṣāʿăqû) and the LORD hears; he delivers them from all their troubles" — ṣāʿaq is the urgent cry of distress, the same verb used for the cry of the Israelites in Egypt (Exodus 2:23; Psalms 3:7, 9) and for the blood of Abel crying from the ground (Genesis 4:10). It is more urgent than ordinary prayer — a cry from a situation of genuine danger or oppression. The pattern established here — the righteous cry, YHWH hears, YHWH delivers — is the structural backbone of the entire Psalter's theology of prayer.
18: "The LORD is close to the brokenhearted (nišbĕrê-lēb) and rescues those whose spirit (rûaḥ) is crushed (dakkāʾ)" — šābar lēb (broken heart) is the condition of one whose inner strength has been destroyed by grief, loss, or oppression. Dakkāʾ (crushed) from dākaʾ (to crush, grind down, pulverize) — the most severe form of spiritual depletion. This verse became a foundational text for understanding divine compassion toward the suffering in Second Temple Judaism and in the NT (Isaiah 57:15; Psalms 61:1 — which Jesus applies to himself in Luke 4:18). The irony: precisely when a person is most broken, God is closest — divine proximity is inversely proportional to human strength.
19: "The righteous face many afflictions (rabbôt rāʿôt)" — the Psalter does not promise immunity from suffering; it promises divine companionship through suffering. This is the antidote to any prosperity-gospel reading of the Psalter. The wisdom tradition is honest about the suffering of the righteous (Job, Ps 73, Lamentations) — the question is not whether suffering occurs but whether YHWH is present within it. "The LORD delivers them from all of them" — the comprehensive deliverance is eschatological as much as historical: the full salvation may await final vindication.
20: "He protects all his bones; not one of them will be broken" — the language of bone-breaking has a specific legal and cultic background. In the Passover law, the bones of the Passover lamb were not to be broken (Exodus 12:46; Numbers 9:12) — the lamb was to be consumed whole, unbroken. The preservation of the righteous person's bones echoes this sacrificial requirement, implicitly comparing the righteous sufferer to the Passover lamb. John 19:36 explicitly cites this verse (or the Passover law it echoes) as fulfilled when the soldiers did not break Jesus's legs. The verse thus stands at the intersection of wisdom ethics and sacrificial typology.
21: "Evil will slay the wicked, and those who hate the righteous will be condemned (yĕʾšāmû)" — the retributive theology of the Psalter: evil is self-destructive. Tišlāḥ rāʿāh rāšāʿ (evil slays the wicked) — the instrument of destruction is the wicked's own evil, which turns on its owner (the boomerang motif of Psalms 7:15–16; Psalms 9:15–16; Psalms 35:7–8). ʾāšam (to be condemned, bear guilt, be guilty) — in the legal sphere, this is the verdict of criminal guilt; in the cultic sphere, it is the state of incurred guilt requiring atonement. For those who hate the righteous, the guilt they incur is their own sentence.
22: "The LORD redeems (pōdeh) the lives of his servants; none who take refuge (ḥōsîm) in him will be condemned" — pādāh (redeem, ransom) in its original context meant buying back something pledged as collateral or a person sold into slavery; it is an economic-legal metaphor for divine rescue. The ḥôsîm (those who take refuge) is a recurring designation for those who trust in YHWH rather than military strength or wealth (Psalms 2:12; Psalms 5:11; Psalms 17:7; Psalms 25:20; Psalms 31:19; Psalms 37:40). "Will not be condemned (lōʾ yeʾšāmû)" — inverts the condemnation of the wicked in Psalms 34:21; the final verse of the acrostic closes the A-to-Z argument: those in YHWH's keeping are not under the condemnation that destroys the wicked.
